title: 2025/10/23 - PP
description: Thursday, 23 October 2025 | Primary Production Briefing on the New Zealand Farm Environment Trust New Zealand Farm Environment Trust Brendan Attrill, National Sustainability Ambassador Sarah Harris, General Manager Paula Attrill,
descriptionSummary: Primary Production. Briefing on the New Zealand Farm Environment Trust. New Zealand Farm Environment Trust

chunks.text: So congratulations on that. Gordon Stevenson tr Yeah, the Gordon Stevenson Trophy is, uh, s is just rewarding. So, uh, we leave it in your hands. Great, thank you. So I'll, um, start. So you, I'm the general manager for the New Zealand Farm Environment Trust.

Um, and our mission, um, as a charity is to champion a good farming and growing practice, um, throughout New Zealand. We do that primarily through our, um, flagship program, the Balanced Farm Environment Awards, which runs through 11 regions of New Zealand. Um, and we are the only pan sector awards in New Zealand, which, um, we think is a really special thing to have.

Um, our entrance go through two rounds of regional judging. Um, and then they, um, select a regional Supreme winner. And at that point we bring them into a national program where we look for an ambassador for that cohort. Um, and we then put 'em through another round of, um, on-farm judging and then bring them into a quickfire interview panel session.

Um, which then, um, allows us to select an ambassador for the, um, recipient of the Gordon Stevenson Trophy, as Sues rightly pointed out, but also we call them our national, um, sustainability ambassador. And in, during this year, um, we were fortunate to have Brendan Natural, um, be awarded with that title. So I won't go on anymore.

I'll hand over to Brendan, um, to tell his story. Okay. Um, just firstly, thanks very much for giving me the opportunity for, um, to actually come down and, and front you. Um, you're, you're both the committees that important a role for the country. Um, so my name's Brendan. I'm married to Susan, and, uh, we farm in Central Taranaki.

Um, so we're, me and Paula are third generation, um, Jerry Farmers, and I just like Paula to, um, do a quick introduction and then I'll, I'll, um, I'll, I'll go into some more detail from Erin. Yeah. So morning everybody. Thanks for the opportunity. Um, obviously Brendan's sister, the older, one of the two siblings.

Um, I'm really pleased to be here as well and support Brendan. I have to get in while we're going, as I just like to Yeah, yeah, yeah. Um, yeah, so really happy to be here to support, um, Brendan's, um, opportunity to speak with you all about what the awards have meant and, and, um, some thoughts about sustainability from the farming context.

The awards are a really big deal, have been a really big deal for our family. So yeah, it's just like the, the, to me, this is what we should be celebrating in New Zealand about the best of what's going on in our rural communities and around production and, um, farming and, um, whatnot. So yeah, really happy to be here and thanks for the opportunity. Okay. So, um, third generation, um, our farming business has been based in central Taranaki.

Um, we're both, uh, mercy graduates. Um, I did ag, um, and postgraduate ag at MSC. Um, Paula did, um, social working. So we've, we started our journey being, um, mom and dad gave us opportunity to learn and problem solve, which is the start of our careers, I suppose. Um, so from an international point of view, my background, I've worked in Europe, um, south America and China, all in the dairy space.

Um, so I come from a quite a very broad, um, background, um, from a New Zealand Inc. Point of view. Um, worked for the New Zealand Dairy Board for a 10 year period early in my career. Um, er as a corporate farming manager, um, looking after all the font own dairy farms in New Zealand, of which, um, David was representing them, um, at the time.

Um, and so I've got a passion for dairy, food and fiber, um, pastoral farming in, in, in New Zealand. Um, and I was also regional science manager, um, in Taranaki for DL for a six month period. Um, so I think I've got quite a broad and unusual, but quite a broad understanding of, um, what's happening in the dairy space.

Um, protein production across the globe and also in, in, um, in New Zealand. Um, as a family, we strongly believe in science. Um, the science will give us the solutions it has done for 150 years in the dairy space and partial farming. Um, and now we've got a really, um, along with being production focused and profit focused, sustainability has really got to be in the forefront of what we do going forward.

Um, if we want our farms to be intergenerational sustainability for myself and Susan, and the family is right in the middle of all our decision making. It was on the outer of our business plan, and we actually brought that right in the center. So, um, all of our decision making is, um, sustainability, um, fact sheet in relation to what we do and, and, and where, where we head.

So our focus along with being profitable and having a good level of productivity is, um, more importantly now around sustainability if we want our farms to be intergenerational. Um, so as a family, there's been a number of building blocks for us in relation to how we've got to where we are now.

So the first thing is operationally you have to be, um, proficient. You have to be, um, know your facts and figures. You have to know the farm systems that you're working in. So that the first part of my career and running the family business was actually, um, bolting on the tools in our toolbox. Um, and, um, so, so being operationally, um, savvy means profit, sustainable farming practices, biodiversity, nutrient sediment management, um, nutrient and nitrogen losses, other business.

So along with being, um, proficient around how we run the businesses. The second thing is have a comprehensive farm plan. Um, that's been a breakthrough for us for the last, um, decade. Um, we're lucky enough that Fonterra won a journey to have a, um, a farm plan incorporated within the sustainability journey. Um, and so farm plans for us have meant, um, that we've been able to, it's been a living document we've been able to achieve, um, some stretch targets.

Um, and we still today look at their document and change it ultra, put stretch in there and make sure that we're, um, you know, we've got the farm position going forward so it can be intergenerational. So part of that farm plan, celebrate the good stuff that's important. Um, and don't sacrifice sustainability wins for productivity gain.

I mean, everyone talks about productivity. Um, they, they should be co-joined and that both of 'em are important for our businesses going forward. Um, the second part of that, um, platform is don't be afraid to lead. Um, generally haven't had an issue with that. Um, sustainability means different things to different people.

Um, so when you're leading provincial and Taranaki, um, around what we are doing, um, you just surround yourself with skilled people, um, who are, uh, focused on sustainability and champions in their field. And that just gives you the energy to, um, keep pushing and, and, and leading. Um, know your numbers is critical.

Um, so if you're going to go down sustainability journey, you're looking for light hanging fruit. And then 10 years into our journey, you're actually looking at the detailed stuff. So you have to know your numbers, you have to be proficient, um, you have to know exactly, um, the impact of changes to your business around sustainability and, and, and where we're heading.

Um, we, our focus is absolute, um, emissions. Um, the, the efficiency space that Fonterra is leading in is important right now, right here, going forward, we'll be judged on total emissions. And so our business, we're focusing on getting down total emissions. And, um, I think over time that will be proven that that will be the direction that the whole country will, will, um, might take a generation, but we need to be moving along that, um, that direction.

Um, our catchments are under pressure across the whole country. And it's one thing that one of the main reasons that I wanted to support Sierra in the competition was to put a lens on, um, the, um, the pressure that catchments are coming under intensive, deering intensive partial farming, um, do does have an impact on our catchments water quality.

And, um, it's just, it's challenging, um, to sell to our communities that, uh, for example, look at the dairy space that, um, so fewer families can have such a wide impact on some of our catchments. So we have to do better in that space. Um, and that's one of the reasons I really wanted to champion, um, what Sierra was doing and putting a lens on on that.

Um, key things around our catchments around water quality, um, sediment, um, movement and into our waters and down through our, um, profiles, nutrient movement, um, potassium, um, nitrogen and, uh, phosphate and also bacteria. Um, so they're all key drivers that at some stage, um, at all different parts around New Zealand, all different catchments, um, they're gonna have an impact.

So front footing, and this is part of Sarah's, um, focus or lens for the whole country, um, is trying to front foot the impact that, um, pastoral farming has on, um, and our catchments. Um, I've had a sheep, we've got had a sheep and beef background.

Mum and dad are originally from tti, or dad was originally from Wai or Nui. Um, and the impact of those communities around what they achieve around partial farming is important and, and they've got a low, um, emissions footprint. The absolute, they've got less levers to pull to actually reduce the footprint than dairy. Um, but it's important that, um, sheep and beef and partial farming in that space is actually protected along the lines as well as dairy and swapping out low intensity farming for high intensity dairy, um, and catchments.

I'm not quite sure that's a direction that communities around New Zealand want us to follow. So along with, um, representing dairy, which one has a strong background in, um, I've got a really strong association with she and beef and their communities and their directions about producing high quality protein. Mm-hmm. So, in summary, um, building blocks sustainable future, um, for food and thrive in New Zealand is tricky.

Um, a growth and expansion model of intensive farm systems, while it puts added pressure on a unique biodiversity and catchments, including sediment, bacteria, nutrient loadings into our waterways and dealing sort profiles, it's something that all industries are grappling with. Um, why I realize that food and fiber are produced on, um, our superb soils and within our pasture based systems is our competitive edge.

Um, it comes a point that our delicate, unique, um, environment, um, just won't be able to actually, um, cope with intensification on some of the catchments and saws that we're, we're, we're working on. Um, we're dependent now more than ever on critical science funding, and I'm a strong follow the science type of person.

Um, and more than ever we, we need, um, all aspects of, um, science and discovery to, uh, work together to identify solutions, particularly around absolute submissions around, um, nutrient movement and bacteria movement into our waterways. So science funding, implementation, and keeping our very skilled, um, science, um, researchers in New Zealand and, and working is very, very important.

Um, there remains a number of low hanging fruits on all farms in New Zealand, including ours. The first thing is to actually achieve those, to actually get our farmers to work towards best practice farm plans, um, you know, Sarah's, um, profile and, and her, um, program, and to identify those low hanging fruits on all our farms to make a shift on the other easy shifts.

We're gonna need science backing up after that to get the detail right. Farm plans are critical for our future. They really put a lens on what we do every day, um, how we operate and how do we actually capture the low hanging fruit around sustainability. Um, so we need to shift, um, and our, our global competitive edge is the production of high quality protein.

We all know that. Um, but that's gonna be intergenerational sustainable for my son, um, and generations ahead. Sustainability has to become a key part of, um, our platforms going forward. So finding a balance between protecting our environment, supporting nature, and building world, beating agribusinesses, um, which are intergenerational, are the challenges ahead.

Thanks very much. Mm. Thanks Brendan. Well, we're gonna go straight into some questions unless Sarah or Paul want to add anything to, alright. I'll just make a quick comment. Miles about, um, so Brendan's talked a lot about sustainability in the context of farming and, and produce and whatnot. I mean, I, I think it, we need to be, I mean, I'm a social worker, right?

So I'm the sort of person who works with kids and families and social problems day in and day out. Um, and I was up in well marketing last weekend for a fundraiser for the local school. And that involved two big stations being opened up and creating a 14 kilometer walking track. That 500 people paid $60 to go and walk across this beautiful countryside.

And the, the, the overarching impression I got from having gone and done the walk and, you know, watch what was going on was sustainability is also about communities. Mm-hmm. And using our natural resources, including our farms and everything, our farming communities and everything they involve, to come together to support future generations.

And it was real, it was a fantastic example of New Zealanders coming together in a rural context using what they have to, to create a sense of community, bring people together, and ultimately contribute to the education of our future generation. So it was awesome. So I guess the thing I would volunteer to the discussion today is that sustainability is all about our farming practices and what we're doing to protect the environment.

It's also about our communities. But yeah. Well Said. Thank you, Simon. Oh, Thank you, miles. Good morning. And thank you Brendan, a really interesting, um, presentation. Um, I have had the pleasure of attending the balanced farm environment rewards a number of times. It's great to see the work that farmers are doing on sustainability. Um, I'd just like to ask you some specific questions about practical measures that designers of freshwater policy should be thinking about to encourage the primary production sector along the same

journey that you've been on. Mm-hmm. Because while we understand there's a lot of really positive stuff happening, um, in primary production around managing discharges from, from, from farm activities, I, I guess the outliers in the industry are the ones that get all the attention. And so I'm interested as to, as to what kind of policies or practical measures you think policy makers should be looking at to start, um, channeling, uh, the, the industry in the direction that you are going in.

Yeah, so that's a, that's a really good question. So the, um, in the short term, the compliance stuff around effluent, that's been tied it up a lot. Um, so if you look across the dairy sector, for example, um, the placement, the effluent, the areas that we're applying to the application rates, the depth of application, there's a real focus on the regional councils now on making sure that that's all land base and no direct water, um, you don't discharge.

So we think as a industry, we're actually making very good progress in that, in that front. Well, in my mind we are, um, we're lucky. Regional council has got a very proactive, um, relationship with farmers. Um, and as a group, we've gone, um, hand in hand with the TRC to actually achieve that on farm.

There is a lot of stuff we can do. Um, if I had to break it down to the, the most crucial things, the crucial things are sediment movement, which picks up, um, phosphate, nitrogen, and potassium. And the second thing is, um, bacteria. So if you looked at the dairy sector, um, specifically the other issues that we, um, need to, need to work on.

So the first thing is, um, we need our catchment communities to be much stronger at the moment. They're doing a very good job, um, but we actually need them stronger, working harder and connecting with more landowners that that's, that's the first step on a farm basis. Um, just operational stuff like, um, for example, you know, if you've got a intensive sheep and beef, um, set up, um, around troughs and, and down, um, entrance way to paddocks and down hedges, for example, there's a lot of positive nutrients.

So, for example, applying fertilizer, um, over and above those areas, all you're doing is intensifying the loss fruit, salt profiles. Mm-hmm. So stuff like, um, using technology to make sure that you don't apply, um, PK and nitrogen onto into, um, around waterways, around, um, those tens of areas.

We stop, stop stock cams. Now a good example of that for us at home, that's, um, reduced our total nutrient, um, input by about 15%. Um, and we've had a positive impact on our net end loss going through our business at home. So just be smart about where we're putting the nutrients. Um, rip, um, charnay is a long way advanced compared to some parts of New Zealand.

What the riparian does is stop the sediment lateral movement, and that's what we're trying to achieve. So, high rainfall areas where a lot of the dairy sectors are actually operating, um, you get rainfall atmos laterally across the soil profile, it picks up sediment and you need a buffer. So the rip is a, has ability to actually create that buffer.

Um, it drops the, the sediment or stops the sediment flow, uh, reduces the speed of the flow, um, and make sure that when it gets to the waterways, it's actually this, a lot of sediment's been captured, filtered and stopped. So keeping riperion going is, um, is, is crucial. Um, and there's some stuff around cows looking at our stocking rates.

Um, we are big pro promoting of reducing stocking rates. Um, Tara's gone through, um, that, that cow numbers have in the eighties have looked at what we were doing in the eighties, which was very high stocking rates, high utilization and pasture low per cow production. Now the industry's headed towards, um, reducing cow numbers higher per cow production, um, less impact on the environment in relation to, particularly around euron spots when they deposit around nitrogen.

Um, so look at your stocking rate, trying to work on, um, getting the, um, the production per cow up so it can run less cows and do the same pro um, productivity targets, um, which then should have a flow on of increasing profitability. Mm. Okay. So that's, that's a, that's a crucial space. There's a limit where that fails.

If you get down to lower stocking rate, then you feed utilization and on-farm performance drops off. So there's a balance that you have to actually achieve to, to go on Brendan. That's fantastic. Um, one question then, because I think you've hit pretty much everything that I would've expected. Yeah. Um, a sustainability advocate to hit. Yeah.

Um, do you think that the government or councils should be telling farmers how many cows they're allowed per hectare and what the stocking rate should be? Or is that something that through your farm plan, through your catchment group, be able to identify or, um, in, in a way that better reflects, um, each farm situation?

In my mind, I think the, the, the first priority is to give the individual industries the opportunity to achieve better, uh, KPIs around sustainability. Mm. Um, so the government supporting, um, industry is, is very, very important. Um, some of those industries are forward looking and fast moving in relation to what they wanna achieve around absolute, um, emissions.

Um, there comes a point though, and every catchment community in New Zealand is different in taranaki, we're very lucky, we've got very good soils, um, where the filtration of our sediment and, um, our nutrients through the soil profile is, um, relatively, um, um, slow other catchments around New Zealand.

You've got a very rapid, um, um, you know, movement of sediment and bacteria and also, um, nutrients through the profiles. So some of those nutrients, I think, uh, some of those catchments are gonna need some type of support because we are looking back now 20 or 30 years and some of our KPIs around, um, nitrogen and water, um, the movement of potassium and, and, um, phosphate, they're actually not improving on some catchments Mm.

Some catchments are making rapid gains. So I think, um, it's a, it's a step-by-step process on individual catchments. So you've got some high risk soils in New Zealand, for example, um, that have got intensive farming practices happening at the moment. Mm-hmm. And, and those, those catchments, for example, may need more support than the likes of the whiteout in Taranaki, where we've got, um, you know, quite significant process progress around sustainable farming practices.

Yeah. Great. Thank you. So as you start on this, Well, you've probably kind of answered it, but thank you very much for being here, and thank you for what you said before. It was really interesting. You talked about the need to strengthen our catchment communities. Mm-hmm. And I just wondered if you had, and you've, you've talked a little bit about it just now, but, um, and the diverse nature of the catchment groups, and I just wondered if you could see a role that we could play or anything that we could do to facilitate this from our end.

Like, okay. So the, so the single biggest industry, which is gonna have the biggest impact on our sustainability, um, KPIs is deer. Okay. That's not duck shovel around that. The, the, the, the, we are the industry and I'm part of it that are having the single biggest impact on, um, sustainability and, and issues relating to water quality, et cetera, et cetera.

Okay. Um, the catchment communities are excellent. The, the actual, um, the actual program is very, very good, but it's not touching enough dairy farmers. Mm. So the single biggest, um, industry which is having the highest impact on sustainable KPIs, um, dairy farmers in general are not, um, connecting with, um, catchment communities.

They're trying, Tara's got one of the best, um, communities in, in the country. It's probably ranked in the top one and two along with Southland. Yeah. Yeah. And, um, we're just struggling to get the dairy sector involved. So I think, um, the government's got a role to play to actually, um, Fonterra and all the other mill processes, open country, some like farm plans are important.

Link those farm plans to the catchment communities, try and get them to actually start engaging more with the catchment communities. 'cause at some point, if we're not seeing movement on, so you're thinking about compulsory Involvement, Not at the start. I think it's gotta be a industry, um, support role that you need to play.

Um, but at some point, our wider 5 million people are not gonna sit there and give us a license to farm without improvement in sustainability, um, triggers. Um, so at some point in the future, and you guys got the role, um, right now, and you'll make the biggest impact for our generation going forward.

Um, we need to see a shift. And so getting our milk processes to, to interact with catchment communities, getting our catchment communities in more reconnected with the dairy sector. 'cause that is the crucial, but at the moment that we're, we're missing. Thank you, Celia. Just a up on this, and thank you very much for coming along.

It's really interesting. Um, certainly been to Glen Eden Farms Open day when they, they were one of the regional winners. Good question. And I haven't got the answers. I mean, from, from my, my perspective. Um, so catchment communities, uh, have been well funded up until last year. Okay. Um, somehow getting that, that linkage in, I'd hate to think that, um, you would be in putting enforcement right at the moment over our industries.

I think you get such a, um, negative feedback from that across our, um, pastoral based, um, food and fiber. Um, but again, it's tricky, uh, because we do need to see some intergenerational change around sustainability and KPI numbers around sediment nutrient movement, um, and bacteria going into our waterways.

That's not gonna change unless some real significant, um, changes to what, how we do and what we, um, you know, what we do. It has has to change. Yeah. Yeah. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Okay. Mm. So at the moment, we are celebrating as an industry per unit efficiency. So if you've got a big intensive dairy farm system five, doing two and a half thousand milk solids, a he deer, they can offset all their emissions against how much ion they're doing.

Their total absolute emissions number looked terrible. And net end loss at home is 60, it was 180, um, 10 years ago. Um, some of those high intensity farming systems have got net netting losses of 200, but we are celebrating them because per unit of output are very efficient.

Now this, this is the big issue for the New Zealand Inc. Because at some point per unit output efficiency KPIs is going to fall over because absolute emissions are not coming down as quick as I think are your want. Mm-hmm. Because if you want to have an impact on our water quality, you have to reduce total emissions.

Absolute emissions have to go down. Net end loss needs to reduce. Now for our own industry for Deering, a lot of our big grantee production profitable farms, uh, in some of those regions. Thanks for coming along. I I see it. 11. I really like your point around the catchment communities. I agree with you. I think what's happened in a lot of areas, and my, the good example is we had so much engagement and then fighting with regional council over what we wanted to do as a cross section of the community that wasn't just farmers.

We had land, uh, you know, holders, we had fishermen, we had everyone involved, and we came to agreement on what, uh, do. And then we'd fight with the regional council over what our plan of attack was because.
